explanation_md: `std::vector<int> v{3}` uses brace initialisation, which selects the initialiser-list constructor. The vector contains one element with value 3. `size()` is 1 and `v[0]` is 3.

prompt_md:
Which of the following is the correct two-step idiom for reading and removing the top of a `std::vector<int>` stack?

a) `int top = v.pop_back();`
b) `v.pop_back(); int top = v.back();`
c) `int top = v.back(); v.pop_back();`
d) `int top = v.front(); v.pop_back();`

options.a: `int top = v.pop_back();` — pop returns the removed element
options.b: `v.pop_back(); int top = v.back();` — remove first, then read
options.c: `int top = v.back(); v.pop_back();` — read then remove
options.d: `int top = v.front(); v.pop_back();` — read front, pop back

explanation_md: `pop_back()` returns void, so option (a) does not compile. Option (b) reads the element *after* removing it — that is the previous second-to-last, not the top. Option (d) reads the front (oldest element), not the top.

prompt_md:
A `std::vector<int> v` has `size() == 3` and `capacity() == 8`. After `v.resize(5)`, what are the new values?

a) `size() == 5`, `capacity() == 5`
b) `size() == 5`, `capacity() == 8` (unchanged)
c) `size() == 5`, `capacity() == 10` (doubled)
d) `size() == 8`, `capacity() == 8`
explanation_md: `resize(5)` grows the size from 3 to 5, value-initialising the two new elements. Because the current capacity (8) is already sufficient, no reallocation occurs and capacity stays at 8. Capacity only changes when size needs to exceed it.
`numSeasons` is 4, so the vector has four slots initialised to 0. `summer` is index 1 (value 30) and `winter` is index 3 (value -5). The loop prints all four values in order: 0 30 0 -5.

prompt_md:
Which of the following is the safest way to iterate a `std::vector<bool>` without a compile error?

a) `for (bool& b : v) { ... }`
b) `for (bool* b = &v[0]; b != &v[v.size()]; ++b) { ... }`
c) `for (auto b : v) { ... }`
d) `for (bool&& b : v) { ... }`

options.a: `for (bool& b : v)` — lvalue reference to each element
options.b: `for (bool* b = &v[0]; ...)` — pointer arithmetic over the buffer
options.c: `for (auto b : v)` — deduce the element type automatically
options.d: `for (bool&& b : v)` — rvalue reference to each element

explanation_md: `std::vector<bool>` elements are proxy objects, not real `bool` values. `bool&` (option a) and `bool&&` (option d) cannot bind to proxies — compile errors. Option (b) takes the address of a bit, which is also invalid. `auto` deduces the proxy type and works transparently.
